1: Rehoboam came to Jerusalem and assembled (wayyaqhēl) the house of Judah and Benjamin — 180,000 baḥûrê milḥāmāh (chosen men of war). bāḥar (to choose, select) produces bāḥûr (chosen man, prime fighter). wayyaqhēl (Hiphil of qāhal: to assemble, convoke) is the vocabulary of the sacred assembly — the same word used for Moses assembling Israel at Sinai (Exodus 35:1) and Solomon assembling Israel for the temple dedication (2 Chronicles 5:2). Rehoboam’s military assembly is described in the language of covenant assembly, underscoring the theological stakes of the civil war he intends to wage.
2: The word of YHWH came (wayhî dĕḇar-YHWH ʾel-šĕmaʿyāh) to Shemaiah the man of God (ʾîš hāʾĕlōhîm). ʾîš hāʾĕlōhîm (man of God) is the fixed prophetic title (also used of Moses in 1 Chronicles 23:14, Elijah in 2 Kings 1:9–13, and many others). Shemaiah’s name (šāmaʿ + yāh) = ‘YHWH has heard’ — the prophet who hears YHWH’s word has a name that encodes YHWH’s receptiveness. A divine hearing-name mediates a divine word about not fighting.
3: ‘Speak to Rehoboam son of Solomon, king of Judah, and to all Israel in Judah and Benjamin.’ The address ‘all Israel in Judah and Benjamin’ is the Chronicler’s consistent claim: the true Israel is constituted by those who remain faithful to the Davidic-temple arrangement, not by the northern numerical majority. The two southern tribes plus the Levites and the faithful who migrate south = the remnant Israel of the Chronicler’s theology.
4: ‘Thus says YHWH: You shall not go up or fight against your brothers (lōʾ-taʿălû wĕlōʾ-tillaḥămû ʿim-ʾăḥêkem). Return every man to his house, for this thing is from me (mēʾittî nĕhyĕtāh haddāḇār hazzeh).’ mēʾittî (from me) + nĕhyĕtāh (has happened, Niphal of hāyāh) = the Chronicler’s divine-causation formula, parallel to 2 Chronicles 10:15. YHWH directly claims authorship of the division. The warriors obey (wayyišmĕʿû): for once, the king and army heed the prophetic word.
5: Rehoboam lived (wayyēšeḇ) in Jerusalem and built (wayyiḇen) fortified cities (ʿārê māṣôr) in Judah. māṣôr (fortress, siege defense) from ṣûr (to besiege, confine). The shift from attempted military reconquest (vv1-4) to defensive fortification marks Rehoboam’s strategic adjustment. The Chronicler presents this as legitimate royal activity — building, rather than fighting — appropriate to the chastened king who listened to the prophet.
6: He built Bethlehem (bêt-leḥem), Etam, Tekoa (tĕqôaʿ). Bethlehem is ‘house of bread’ (food production city). Tekoa is the home of the prophet Amos (Amos 1:1) and of the wise woman of 2 Sam 14. The specific city selection in the list follows a geographic logic (from Jerusalem southward into the Shephelah and Negev), representing the full defensive perimeter of Judah.
7: Beth-zur (bêt-ṣûr), Soco (śōkôh), Adullam (ʿădullām). Beth-zur = house of rock/strength; Adullam = the site of David’s cave refuge (1 Samuel 22:1) — a location already associated with royal protection in extremity. The cities of the Shephelah control the western approaches into Judah from the coastal plain.
8: Gath (gat), Mareshah (mārēšāh), Ziph (zîp). Gath = the former Philistine city; its fortification by Rehoboam signals the extension of Judahite control over historically Philistine territory. Mareshah features prominently later (2 Chronicles 14:9-10 = the battle against Zerah the Cushite). The cities represent frontier defense of the western and southwestern borders.
9: Adoraim (ʾăḏôrāyim), Lachish (lāḵîš), Azekah (ʿăzēqāh). Lachish is the most significant fortified city in the list — a major administrative and military center that Assyria would besiege in 701 BC (2 Chronicles 32:9; Isaiah 36:2; the Lachish reliefs of Sennacherib). Azekah is also significant (Jeremiah 34:7). The Chronicler’s list anticipates subsequent military geography.
10: Zorah (ṣorʿāh), Aijalon (ʾayyālôn), Hebron (ḥeḇrôn) — which were in Judah and Benjamin, as fortified cities. Zorah is Samson’s hometown (Judges 13:2). Aijalon is where the sun stood still (Joshua 10:12). Hebron is the original Davidic capital (2 Samuel 2:1-4). The list of fortified cities includes places of earlier covenant memory, suggesting that Rehoboam is not merely building strategically but reclaiming the full geographic inheritance of the Davidic covenant.
11: He reinforced (wayyĕḥazzēq) the strongholds and put commanders (nĕgîḏîm) in them, with food stores, oil, and wine. ḥāzaq (Piel: to strengthen, reinforce) is the Chronicler’s characteristic word for consolidating what is threatened. nāgîḏ (prince, leader) is the term used for Saul’s designation (1 Samuel 9:16) and for the leaders of the northern migration in this chapter (v22). The word signals royal delegation of authority in the defensive network.
12: In every city, shields (māginnîm) and spears (ḥănîṯîm) were placed, and he made them very strong (wayyĕḥazzĕqēm lĕmĕʾōḏ). Rehoboam held Judah and Benjamin. lĕmĕʾōḏ (very much, exceedingly) as an intensifier. The physical fortification list (cities, commanders, provisions, weapons) is the Chronicler’s portrait of a king who, after the prophetic chastening, channels his energy into legitimate defense rather than fratricidal civil war.
13: The priests and Levites who were in all Israel (kol-yiśrāʾēl) presented themselves to Rehoboam from all their territories. hityaṣṣĕḇû ʿālāyw (they stationed themselves / presented themselves to him) — the Hithpael of yāṣaḇ (reflexive: to take one’s stand, present oneself). This verse introduces the great Levitical migration south. The Chronicler’s point: the legitimate cultic order cannot function in the north under Jeroboam’s system and therefore abandons it.
14: The Levites left their pasturelands (migrĕšêhem) and their property (ʾăḥuzzātām) and came to Judah and Jerusalem. migrāš (pastureland, common land around a Levitical city) is the technical term from Numbers 35:2-5 for the Levitical territories. ʾăḥuzzāh (possession, property) is the land-holding term of the Promised Land distribution. The Levites abandon their territorial claims in the north — a profound economic sacrifice — because Jeroboam had rejected them from being priests (mikkahēn lĕKHWH).
15: Jeroboam had appointed (wayyaʿămēḏ) his own priests for the high places (labbāmôt), for the goat demons (laśśĕʿîrîm), and for the calves (lāʿĕgālîm) that he had made. śāʿîr (goat, goat demon) appears in the prohibition of Leviticus 17:7 against ‘sacrificing to goat demons’ — the same word used here. ʿēgel (calf) = the golden calves of Bethel and Dan (1 Kings 12:28–29), recapitulating the Sinai apostasy. The Chronicler’s characterization of the northern cult as demon-and-calf worship is polemical but uses the legal vocabulary of Lev 17–18.
16: ‘And after the Levites, those from all the tribes of Israel who set their hearts (nātnû ʾet-libbām) to seek YHWH (liḏrōš ʾet-YHWH) came to Jerusalem.’ nātan lēḇ (to give/set the heart) = to devote oneself, commit intentionally. dāraš (to seek, inquire) is the Chronicler’s central term for covenantal faithfulness: to seek YHWH is the opposite of forsaking him. The faithful remnant from all northern tribes self-selects by the criterion of seeking YHWH — the Chronicler’s pan-Israel theology in miniature.
17: ‘They strengthened (yĕḥazzĕqû) the kingdom of Judah and supported Rehoboam son of Solomon for three years.’ ḥāzaq (Piel) again — the migration of the faithful strengthens the Davidic kingdom precisely. The three-year period of obedience links to v17’s explanation: ‘for three years they walked in the way of David and Solomon’ (hāleḵû bĕdereḵ dāwîḏ wĕšĕlōmōh). The spatial metaphor of walking (hālaḵ) in the right way is Deuteronomic covenant language (Deuteronomy 5:33; 2 Chronicles 8:6).
18: Rehoboam took as wife Mahalath (maḥălat), daughter of Jerimoth son of David and of Abihail daughter of Eliab son of Jesse. The genealogy is complex: Mahalath’s father Jerimoth is a son of David not mentioned in the main Davidic genealogy (1 Chr 3), suggesting a concubine-born son. Abihail her mother is granddaughter of Eliab, David’s eldest brother (1 Samuel 16:6). The marriage ties Rehoboam tightly to the Davidic and Eliadite lines — a strategic endogamous union.
19: She bore him sons: Jeush (yeʿûš), Shemariah (šĕmaryāh), and Zaham (zaham). Three sons from the first wife. The names: yeʿûš (possibly = ‘let him help’ or related to ʾûš); šĕmaryāh = ‘YHWH has preserved’; zaham = perhaps ‘loathing’ — an unusual name whose etymology is uncertain. The Chronicler preserves these names as part of the Davidic genealogical record, not because they play further narrative roles.
20: After Mahalath he took Maacah daughter of Absalom (maʿăḵāh bat-ʾaḇšālôm). Maacah is the daughter of Absalom — David’s rebellious son. A son of Rehoboam marrying a granddaughter of Absalom’s line is politically provocative, given Absalom’s attempted usurpation of the throne. She bore him Abijah, Attai, Ziza, and Shelomith.
21: ‘Rehoboam loved (ʾāhēḇ) Maacah daughter of Absalom more than all his wives and concubines.’ ʾāhēḇ (Qal perfect: loved) as marital/preferential attachment. The same verb is used of Isaac’s preference for Esau in Genesis 25:28 and Jacob’s love for Rachel in Genesis 29:18 — always preference that creates dynastic complications. ‘He had 18 wives and 60 concubines, and fathered 28 sons and 60 daughters.’ The multiplication of wives and concubines violates Deuteronomy 17:17 (the king shall not ‘multiply wives for himself’); the Chronicler reports without editorial comment.
22: Rehoboam made Abijah (ʾăḇîyāh) the head (lĕrōʾš) above his brothers as prince-designate (nāgîḏ). nāgîḏ is the proto-kingship title used at key moments of dynastic designation: Saul (1 Samuel 9:16), David (1 Samuel 13:14; 2 Samuel 6:21), and now Abijah. The appointment of Abijah despite Maacah’s non-first-wife status is the royal prerogative, paralleling David’s choice of Solomon over elder sons. The dynasty maintains the pattern of election-over-primogeniture.
23: Rehoboam ‘acted wisely’ (wayyāśeḵel) in distributing his sons throughout the regions of Judah and Benjamin, to all the fortified cities, giving them abundant provisions and seeking many wives for them. śāḵal (Hiphil: to act wisely, be successful) is a wisdom-and-success verb (the same word as Joseph’s success in Genesis 39:3). Distributing princes to the fortified cities served both a defensive purpose (royal presence in the frontiers) and prevented court concentration of rival claimants. Seeking many wives for them parallels Rehoboam’s own marriage politics.
